concept_seed.one_liner: Editorial gemology reference database with mineral specimen pages, gem grading charts, cutting-style galleries, geological origin maps, certification guides, and collector glossaries; jewel-toned dark luxurious UI with rich visual density, curated collection-style navigation, hover-triggered color-shift animations on gem classification cards, and a refined browsing experience spanning eight detailed reference sections.

metadata.animation_target_hint: gem classification card background and border color transitioning through a jewel-tone palette shift
metadata.animation_side_effects: none — card remains in DOM, visible, no scroll or layout change

accepted_concept.site_goal: Serve as a curated editorial reference database for gem collectors, jewelry designers, and gemology students, providing authoritative classification, grading, and visual reference across all major gem species
accepted_concept.description: A jewel-toned dark-luxury editorial reference site presenting gemological knowledge as a collector's archive. Deep navy and charcoal page backgrounds contrast with saturated gemstone accent colors — emerald, sapphire, ruby, amethyst, citrine — applied per gem family. Classification cards fill dense grid layouts, each card subtly keyed to its gem's color family. Navigation follows a curated collection model with a persistent side rail and breadcrumb trails through eight detailed reference sections.
accepted_concept.motif: jewel-toned dark luxury editorial — deep navy and charcoal backgrounds (#1c1c2e base) with saturated gemstone accent colors shifting by gem family, gold rule dividers, serif specimen labels, and editorial pull-quote typography

pages.layout_pattern: full-width hero with featured specimen spotlight + horizontal curated card strip + editorial gem-of-the-week panel
sections: hero featured specimen with large photograph and classification badge
gem of the week editorial spotlight
quick-navigation taxonomy grid by mineral class

pages.layout_pattern: filterable 4-column classification card grid with left sidebar facet filters by mineral system, hardness, crystal structure, and gem family
sections: filter sidebar with collapsible facet groups

pages.layout_pattern: tabbed reference panels per gem type with full-width comparison charts, visual grading scales, and certification equivalency matrix
sections: clarity grading chart with inclusion diagrams

pages.layout_pattern: masonry gallery grid with slide-in detail drawer showing SVG facet diagrams, proportions, and recommended gem types
sections: masonry gallery grid of cut illustrations
facet count and angle reference table
historical development and regional origin notes

pages.layout_pattern: full-bleed interactive SVG world map with clickable deposit markers and collapsible regional detail panel on the right
sections: world map canvas with deposit point markers
deposit type and gem family legend
regional detail panel with mine profiles

pages.layout_pattern: alphabetical sticky index rail on left with scrollable definition entries, inline cross-reference links, and search input at top
sections: definition entry list with gem family tags

pages.layout_pattern: structured guide pages with multi-column lab comparison table, lab profile cards, and treatment disclosure reference
sections: certification overview and why it matters
major lab comparison table (GIA, AGL, Gübelin, SSEF)
how to read a grading report guide
treatment disclosure standards by gem type

pages.layout_pattern: editorial long-form single-column with pull quotes, contributor bios, and sourcing methodology notes

message_intent: establish authoritative expertise in gemological reference
make complex classification data visually accessible and browsable
evoke the luxury and rarity of fine gemstones through UI aesthetic
support both casual collector browsing and deep gemologist research
content_model: GemSpecimen: name, species, variety, trade names, hardness, crystal system, refractive index, specific gravity, treatment status, origin regions, images
CutStyle: name, category, facet count, crown angle, pavilion angle, SVG diagram, best gem types, historical notes
GradingScale: gem type, grading authority, color descriptor scale, clarity grade map, cut proportion ranges
GlossaryTerm: term, definition, etymology, gem families, related term links
OriginDeposit: country, region, coordinates, gem types, geological period, deposit type, notable mines
interactions: facet filter selection and reset on mineral index sidebar
gem type tab switching on grading page
deposit marker click revealing regional detail panel on origins map
cut gallery masonry card opening detail drawer
hover jewel-tone color-shift on gem classification cards

animations.description: On mouseenter, the gem classification card's CSS background-color transitions from deep charcoal (#1c1c2e) to a rich jewel tone keyed to the card's data-gem-family attribute — #0d3d2e for beryl, #0e1f4a for blue corundum, #3d0e1a for red corundum, #2a0e4a for amethyst, #3d2e00 for chrysoberyl — while border-color transitions from #3a3a4a to the gem's saturated accent (#2ecc71, #3498db, #e74c3c, #9b59b6, #f1c40f respectively); timing is ease-in-out over 600ms; on mouseleave all colors return to base values over the same duration; card position, size, and page layout remain entirely unchanged throughout

asset_needs: SVG world map of major gem deposit locations with coordinate data
SVG facet diagram illustrations for 20+ cut styles
high-resolution mineral specimen photographs per gem species
grading scale gradient graphics for color and clarity
gem family color palette design tokens
accepted_concept.mobile_behavior: single-column stacked layout; bottom-sheet filter drawer replaces sidebar; origins map switches to searchable regional list; masonry gallery becomes 2-column

strengths: Animation precisely matches seed spec: color channel, hover trigger, 600ms within 300–900ms window, background and border transitions keyed per gem family with exact hex values (#0d3d2e beryl, #0e1f4a blue corundum, #3d0e1a red corundum, #2a0e4a amethyst, #3d2e00 chrysoberyl), no layout change
Motif is exactly what the seed specifies — jewel-toned dark luxury editorial — with #1c1c2e base, saturated gem-family accent colors, gold rule dividers, serif specimen labels

All 8 pages have distinct, non-duplicate layout patterns: hero+strip, 4-column filterable grid, tabbed comparison charts, masonry+drawer, full-bleed SVG map, sticky A-Z rail, multi-column lab table, editorial long-form
Interactions are concrete and varied: facet sidebar filter, gem type tab switch, deposit marker click, letter jump nav, masonry drawer, hover color-shift
Audience (collectors, students, designers, appraisers) aligns with seed's curated-collection browsing intent rather than tilting purely professional

Content model includes treatment status and trade names on GemSpecimen, multi-lab certification equivalency matrix on grading page, and real labs named (GIA, AGL, Gübelin, SSEF) — domain specificity is high
weaknesses: About page sections are competent but relatively standard (mission, bios, methodology, revision log) compared to the richness of other pages
Masonry layout for cutting gallery is common; a faceted-diagram grid with a pinned comparison panel would be more differentiated

strengths: Content model is the most technically rigorous of the two: IMA-recognized naming, chemical formula, type locality, report verification URLs, jurisdiction acceptance, average turnaround — credible professional depth
Origins map page adds a geological period timeline range filter and gem-type overlay toggle, interactions not present in the other candidate and genuinely useful for research workflows
Lab-guides page with side-by-side scrollable comparison columns and report format sample panels is concrete and differentiated
Taxonomy tree sidebar persisting across pages creates a coherent hierarchical navigation model appropriate to the domain
weaknesses: Animation channel is motion (translateY + scale lift), not color — a direct mismatch with the seed's animation_channel: 'color' and the explicit requirement for hover-triggered color-shift on gem classification cards
Warm obsidian + walnut-brown + burnished gold motif diverges from the seed's jewel-toned dark luxury specification; this is a different aesthetic register, not a refinement of it
Audience skewed to professional gemologists and lab technicians rather than the seed's collector/student browsing experience, reducing alignment with the curated-collection navigation intent
Cut card hover lift is a generic e-commerce pattern that does not carry the domain specificity of jewel-tone color shift

issues.message: The glossary page's layout consistency score is near-zero (2.75/100), with 98 of 105 measured elements flagged as lacking horizontal alignment. While the content is rich and the letter-section headers are visible, this extreme score suggests the A-Z sticky letter rail on the left may not be implemented as a true fixed side rail — the tool sees the rail offsets and the content column as unrelated baselines. Verify that the letter nav is using 'position: sticky' so it stays anchored during scroll and is visually separated from (but aligned beside) the definition entries.

The automated component consistency score for the mineral index card grid is very low (~16/100). Visually the cards look uniform, but the static analysis is tripping over nested sub-elements inside each card — specifically the property/value rows (hardness, crystal system, RI, SG, origin) where some cells are bare divs with a single child rather than consistently structured label-value pairs. Standardising the inner DOM of each data row (e.g. consistently wrapping every property label in a 'span.label' and its value in a 'span.value' inside a shared row div) would resolve the structural mismatches the tool flags.

The cutting-styles gallery cards score ~13/100 for component style consistency. The gallery outer cards (12 items) are visually consistent, but the SVG facet-diagram container elements nested inside each article card vary in child count and tag structure across entries — some have only the SVG icon with no sibling wrapper, while others have wrapping div containers. Normalize each cut card so the facet-diagram zone, cut-name div, and detail footer all use the same tag hierarchy across all 12 (and any additional) gallery items.

One revision-log entry in the 'Revision Log' section uses a different internal tag structure from the others (the majority pattern is a title div followed by a body paragraph; one entry wraps its content differently). This causes a minor component consistency flag (87/100). Inspect the revision log entries in about.html and ensure every log item uses the same two-child structure: one div for the date/heading and one paragraph for the description.

The origins page has a noticeable blank region in the lower-right quadrant below the map canvas (sparsity score 89.9, with a ~648k-pixel blank rectangle detected). Consider filling this area with the deposit-type legend, a gem-family legend, or expanding the regional deposit narratives section upward so the map and content sections share vertical space more evenly.

repair_instructions: On the glossary page (glossary.html), give the A-Z letter navigation list 'position: sticky; top: 1rem;' (or equivalent) so it remains fixed in the left rail as the user scrolls through the 8,900px+ page. Also confirm the page uses a two-column CSS grid or flexbox layout so the letter rail occupies the left column and the definition entries the right — if the current implementation is single-column with letters inline, restructure it to the sidebar-plus-content pattern the concept specifies.

In the mineral index (minerals.html), normalise the inner DOM of every gem classification card's property rows. Each physical-property entry (Hardness, Crystal, RI, SG, Origin) should follow a consistent two-child pattern: a 'span' or 'div' with class 'prop-label' and a sibling 'span' or 'div' with class 'prop-value'. Remove any wrapper divs that have only a single child and no sibling, as these are what the consistency checker flags as structurally mismatched.

In the cutting-styles gallery (cuts.html), standardise every cut-style card's internal structure so the SVG facet diagram, the text block (cut name + description), and the metadata footer (facet count + style badge) each sit in their own consistently-named child div. Currently some article cards nest the SVG directly as a solo child without a sibling wrapper; wrap the SVG in a 'div.cut-diagram' container in all cards so the three-part hierarchy is uniform across all gallery items.

In about.html, open the Revision Log section and inspect each log entry. The majority structure is a two-child card: one div for the date-stamped heading and one paragraph for the revision description. One entry currently wraps its content differently (likely an extra nesting level or a different element tag). Update that entry to match the two-child pattern used by the other log items.

On the origins page (origins.html), address the large blank area in the lower-right below the SVG map. Options: (a) extend the deposit-type legend and gem-family legend into that space as a two-column panel; (b) add a 'Currently Selected Region' info box that shows placeholder text when no marker is active; or (c) move the 'Notable Deposit Narratives' section up so it sits beside or immediately below the map rather than leaving a wide empty gap.
